content:
`And for iOS, you can precompile shaders to make animations smoother when they’re run for the first time.`

This is from the Flutter 2.2 release notes as shown above. How does this differ from what already exists?
@Hixie can you confirm whether this is now available in Flutter 2.2 and if so, how to do it? Thank you!
/cc @csells Metal shader warm-up is only available on the dev channel at this point. See the discussion on https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/79298.
Will it be available on Windows applications?

Will the Metal shader warm-up also implement caching similar to Android?

If not, can Flutter on iOS at least try to cache shaders for the duration of that version of the app rather than having to re-build the shaders on each cold launch? Or could a plugin implement this sort of SkSL caching?

Yes, I'm aware of `--cache-sksl`, but it'd be nice if Flutter could help automate some of these.
A one-off performance hit after each install would be far preferred to the jank after each cold start, especially given that we can't guarantee our app won't be randomly killed off while in the background.
Any news on when it will be available on stable for iOS?
https://github.com/flutter/engine/commit/7802d5843de32abf240117f5fd595840a43612dc is available on beta channel since 2.3 and will be in the 2.5 stable.
Thank you very much @zanderso :)
Thanks @zanderso for updating us. Do you know when 2.5 is expected to be released in stable?

I believe an exact date has not been set, but 2.5 is already on beta channel, and it will be promoted to stable before end of Q3. You can read more about our release process on the wiki at https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Flutter-build-release-channels.

I tried the shader caching but unfortunately it doesn't help I still get shader compilation lags.

I am using the dev channel (2.5.0-6.0.pre).
Test-Device: iPhone 12 Pro Max

My steps:
1. flutter clean
2. flutter run --profile --cache-sksl --purge-persistent-cache
3. press M
3. flutter run --profile --bundle-sksl-path flutter_1.sksl.json

Nevertheless the animations lag at the 1st run (sometimes even at the 2nd run). After latest 3rd time all animations are smooth.

Are there any things that still need to be considered? My Flutter project is already over 1 year old. Have any files been changed that are needed for shader caching?

@b3nni97 I am quite surprised it does not work for you. For me it worked great so far (on iOS even better than Android).
Did you already try to run the 4th command in release mode? This looks like the only difference right now.
@FelixMittermeier  whether with --profile or --release are both times the same result
@b3nni97 This is really strange. Just to make sure: you triggered all the animations in your app between step 2. and 3. right?

@FelixMittermeier Yes, of course. 

It also only happens when I manually delete the app after Profile mode. If I just start with --release/--profile (without deleting the app manually before) then there are no lags either.

How big is your .json file? Maybe the problem lies somewhere there.

I just see that after the command:
flutter run --profile --bundle-sksl-path flutter_1.sksl.json

These messages appear:
`Xcode build done.                                           98,6s
(lldb) 2021-08-18 22:01:48.763875+0200 Runner[9517:4140609] fopen failed for data file: errno = 2 (No such file or directory)
Errors found! Invalidating cache...
Warning: Unable to create restoration in progress marker file
fopen failed for data file: errno = 2 (No such file or directory)
Errors found! Invalidating cache...`

Is this normal or is this the problem?

If the animations you are precaching are particularly prone to involving a lot of shaders, consider setting timeDilation to a high number (e.g. 100.0) when doing the precaching run. Sometimes what happens is that the shaders take so long to compile on the training run that we actually miss frames that themselves are going to involve more shaders. There's work ongoing in the Skia team to reduce that kind of problem by reducing the number of factors that introduce a new shader, so this should be less of an issue eventually.

> 
> I am using the dev channel (2.5.0-6.0.pre).
> Test-Device: iPhone 12 Pro Max

> 
> 1. flutter clean
> 2. flutter run --profile --cache-sksl --purge-persistent-cache
> 3. press M
> 4. flutter run --profile --bundle-sksl-path flutter_1.sksl.json

> Are there any things that still need to be considered? My Flutter project is already over 1 year old. Have any files been changed that are needed for shader caching?

As I know, it is not possible to run the app locally using preached shaders. You can only build release apk/appbundle/ipa etc and include that sksl.json file.

@Hixie  I have now tried with the timeDilation. Unfortunately this has brought nothing. 2-3 frames within the animation are skipped anyway. It seems as if the caching does not work at all. 

These two commands have exactly the same lags:
flutter build ios --bundle-sksl-path flutter_01.sksl.json
flutter build ios 

Are there any known widgets that prevent precaching? (BackdropFilter, RepaintBoundary, FadeTransition, etc?)
Or is there some way to debug this?


@alectogeek  I tried both commands and got exactly the same result.
I'm experiencing the same lack of improvements as @b3nni97 , ranging from iphone 6 to X. It just appears not to be working at all.

@b3nni97 I'm curious about how you're measuring any benefit from that. Does your application perform worse when you do not use the `shaderWarmUp`? If you try to `--cache-sksl` from a run that _does_ use that shader warmup routine, do you still need it when you use the cached sksl?
Also - is the code you pasted in the same as the code you're using in your application later to draw things?

> @b3nni97 I'm curious about how you're measuring any benefit from that. Does your application perform worse when you do not use the `shaderWarmUp`? If you try to `--cache-sksl` from a run that _does_ use that shader warmup routine, do you still need it when you use the cached sksl?

Hi. Will this thing be available for desktop applications?
@b3nni97 If you wouldn't mind, your report is a bit concerning to us, so splitting it out into a separate GitHub issue with the details that @dnfield is asking for would be very helpful. Thanks!
It would be super helpful if you can create a demo that does this, including a `flutter_driver` test that runs through the workflow to generate the shaders.

@dnfield I can give you access to my private repository. Sorry, I don't have the capacity to put this into a small example. 

@fabiancrx I use the latest master (Channel master, 2.6.0-1.0.pre.46)
I am not able to look at private repositories for this. I'm sorry. Please feel free to file a new issue if you're able to get a smaller example of this that can be publicly shared.

Current status: Shader warm-up is available on every platform. To automate the generation of shader warm-up files currently requires first manually writing automated tests, and then running these in CI.

Our medium-term efforts are around removing the need for shader warm-up entirely, and therefore we are not currently planning on working on further automating the creation of shader warm-up files (no point working on something that we want to make irrelevant).

If we manage to remove the need for shader warm-up entirely, we will close this issue. If not, we will reconsider whether additional efforts to automate shader warm-up file generation are warranted.
@Hixie is there a way to track the progress of that work (removing the need for shader warm-up)?
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/77412 is where that work is being tracked.
@Hixie But how would run these tests in CI? The profile mode requires a physical device

You can build the warm-up shaders in debug mode, but using a physical device is preferred regardless. I don't have a good answer for how to test devices in CI short of having your own custom CI or using something like Firebase Test Lab (I've no idea if FTL supports this use case though, I must admit).

There's a major caveat here: there are still cases where warming up shaders on a particular device will result in shaders that are not usable on some other device, e.g. if your warmup shaders use OpenGL features available when created but then are not available on some older/different GPU. There is no solution for this currently.

Looks like shader warmup files are written in SKSL format that is independent of  underlying GLSL or Metal implementation. 
So wonder why there are cases of different results for iOS or Android or opengl features

The SkSL can encode information that, when translated to GLSL for example, will use features that the GPU it was created on had but the GPU it's running on currently do not. SkSL is agnostic to platform but it is not (currently) agnostic to feature detection and usage - Skia currently assumes that if a feature was detected at generation, it can just use that. 

In th best case, the shader will fail to compile and a new one will be created and compiled if this happens. If we want to further pursue this solution we'll have to come up with something better for that case.

Has there been any consideration to doing this in more of a JIT way?

Historically, on platforms like Unity or Flash, we have solved this issue by showing some invisible components in frame 1, while our splash screen is showing, so their shaders/textures are uploaded to gpu. Then, when they appear in the app for the first time, they are already cached and ready to go, everything is 60fps butter. Users don't notice anything, except maybe a small stutter on the preload animation and an extra 100-500ms of startup time.

Seems like we could have a widget, that could take a stack of items, and cache all the shaders created from those items. Then write those down to disk. You could have a full 1000ms to do this, it would be no problem, and developers would happily make that tradeoff. 

Then all of this complexity becomes something as simple as sticking a widget on your splash page, with some child widgets, and waiting until its cache is complete. You also would get device-specific shaders this way, which seems simpler than dealing with myriad edge cases. It also does not bloat the file size of the download.

Obviously developers would need to be careful with views that initiate network calls or other actions when mounted, but that is a much easier problem to solve, then all of this crazy build process and bundling complexity.

Yes, that has been considered, but it turns out that it's very difficult to capture just the right shaders that Skia will create for the specific things your app will draw. This gets very difficult to maintain, and it slows down startup time which our customers care about.

no solution yet? I started building with flutter desktop and noticed the transition jank of animations way later.

been years with this issue right?

They have tried various unsatisfying hacks like profile-guided precompilation, but it looks like they're finally started working on a real solution:

[Impeller](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Impeller) is a replacement for Skia that won't dynamically construct and compile shaders at runtime. Not at all ready yet but I'm very glad they've finally bit the bullet. The end is in sight at least!
SkSL caching and warm-up is no longer the approach the team is pursuing to solve early-onset jank.

What's the plan?

Thanks.

Our new graphics backend, [Impeller](https://docs.flutter.dev/perf/impeller), doesn't compile shaders on startup (it doesn't compile them at runtime at all), so there's no need to warm up shaders any more once Impeller is available. So the plan is to get Impeller working on as many platforms as we can. It's on by default on iOS now, Android is next, then we'll look at the desktop platforms. macOS should be relatively easy. Windows might be tricky because of DirectX. I forget the state of Linux and web regarding Impeller.

Once Impeller is more widely deployed we'll remove the old shader warm-up logic (see https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/132418).
Can we continue to use sksl warmup in the meantime or does it not have any effect for iOS now?

## Restoration Framework

Marked as WIP because I am still working on the tests (I decided to refactor the original testing framework for state restoration a little more to make it easier to use).

## Description

Implements https://flutter.dev/go/state-restoration-design.

This PR introduces the basic framework for state restoration in Flutter as outlined in the document above.

In the service layer, the following concepts are introduced:

* `RestorationManager`, which synchronizes the restoration data between engine and framework.
* `RestorationBucket`, which stores the restoration data in a tree.
* `RestorationId`, which identifies a piece of restoration data uniquely within a `RestorationBucket`.

In the widget layer, the following concepts are introduced. This is what most developers will presumably work with to make their apps restorable.

* `RestorationMixin`, which is mixed into a `State` object to make certain values in the state restorable.
* `RestorableProperty`, which holds the data within a `State` object that need to be restorable.
* Many subclasses of `RestorableProperty` for storing specific data types, e.g. `RestorableInt`, `RestorableString`, etc.
* Various `RestorationScope` widgets to create new name spaces for `RestorationId`s in the widget tree.

## Related Issues

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/6827

## Tests

I added the following tests:

* Tests for newly introduced concepts in the service layer: `RestorationManager`, `RestorationBucket`, `RestorationId` and friends.
* WIP: tests for the widget layer.

- [x] The analyzer (`flutter analyze --flutter-repo`) does not report any problems on my PR.
- [x] I am willing to follow-up on review comments in a timely manner.

## Breaking Change
